JavaScript Core (53) - Inheritance & the Prototype Chain - Chapter Exercise
This chapter is all about practice.
When a concept hasn't quite clicked yet, drilling it with lots of practice, sharing it with others, or even teaching it can really strengthen how well you understand it.
So here's the exercise for this chapter:

Conditions:

Must use Object.create
The structure must be correct
Each prototype needs its own methods
For this one I'm going to use the online game Ragnarok Online as my example (yeah, I'm showing my age).

// Every starting character has these basic skills

'Lv 2 使用表情符號。可用 Alt + 0 ~ 9 及 Ctrl + 1 、 - 、 = 、
鍵表示感情。可按 Alt + L 鍵能使用更多的表情符號。'
'Lv 3 可在原地坐下使恢復速度增加。可按 Insert 鍵或使用 / 坐下的指令。'
'Lv 4 可以開設聊天室。可按 Alt + C 鍵。'
'Lv 5 可以加入其它玩家的隊伍中。'
'Lv 6 使用卡普拉職員倉庫保管道具。'
'Lv 7 可以組隊。使用 / ORGANIZE "組隊名稱"的指令。可按 Alt + Z 鍵調整隊伍選項。'
// Move and attack — abilities every starting character has
Practicing with concepts you're already comfortable with — and gamifying it a bit — really does make it more fun to drill.
Novice (I tweaked the code a little, so this image is just an illustration)
Now, in a game like this, you eventually do a job change. So you inherit your original Novice skills and switch into a specific class. Here I'll keep going using my all-time favorite, the WhiteSmith.
// After a job change your stats definitely change
// After a job change, damage, HP, and job all change, so we have to pass them in again
// the cart attack skill needs Cart Boost active before you can attack
// The WhiteSmith also inherits the abilities of the starting character
// Back to normal after five seconds
Now let's call the relevant methods again:

Of course, the prototype chain above is just an exercise, so the way I split it up probably isn't perfect. The main point is to use something I've actually played around with before to make it more interesting.
One last thing to watch out for. If you put
brings over. So when you write prototypes, pay close attention to the order of your code.

This chapter is all about practice. When a concept hasn't quite clicked, drilling it with lots of hands-on practice, sharing, or even teaching it can really cement your understanding. Here I build out an inheritance and prototype-chain example using the old online game Ragnarok Online — base character, novice skills, and a job change into the WhiteSmith class.
